speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 4. Survivor is Evy Woods. Tape number 5-- I'm correcting that. Tape number 5. Survivor is Evy Woods. The date is April 16, 1996. We left off on the last tape in-- correct my pronunciation-- Gamsau? Am I--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gamsau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gamsau. You were with your mother. What is happening? What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was, on and off, still sick all the time, and it was no fun going to the outhouse in the middle of the night in this winter. It's by now December, you know, slash January '44 to '45. And I play with the children. There were certainly assortments of them. And they were not inquisitive children. They knew of no world but their sharecropper world. And even the Nazi daughter-in-law was not a problem. She was more concerned about her two children. So things were relatively easy, except for the periodic visitor and the mailman and the whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We started to prepare the cellar, because we knew-- that potato cellar there-- we knew we would have to go in there because we were not going to force-- be forced by the Germans to evacuate, as had the Bunkes in the other location.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mama Subitski had no intentions of doing it. So she put up the horse and cart and made it look like that. And whenever they would come around to say, well, are you-- are you loading? Are you going? She'd say, see, we're ready. We're-- we're getting there. We're packing. She had no intent of leaving her-- her little house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the cellar was by now stocked with some stuff in addition to potatoes. It had onions, things to drink. And we would-- we already had experience at being in the cellar, my mother and I, but they did not. And we would go down there and sit and spend time in-- with the candles so that we would know what to expect of ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were so many people and so many small children, smaller than I was, two and three-year-olds, you know? And of course, everyone had heard about the Russians, the barbarians. So we obviously had to hide. I think there was one 15-year-old-- 14-year-old boy, her son, on hand, and that was the only reasonable male who could-- he's not going to be able to protect all these people. The day finally came-- or the week came that we finally had to go into the cellar and stay there, and we would stay there mostly during the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, up the road from the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's not a street. This is just dirt, you know, dirt road, where the street was that's called in German, the chaussee. Chaussee is like a highway before there were highways-- the main street from one city to another, the connecting road, but a halfway decent road. That road was filled with wall-to-wall people all trying to go westward, as they were told to do and were-- it was a law they had to obey. You know, destroy everything you leave behind and move. So the street was filled with that. And on some days, people would stop and ask for water, because they had walked for who knows how many cities away or villages away. And they would try to give them what they could. But by now disease was happening with these people. And by now the bombs had fallen even here and there. And the water wasn't any good, had to boil that. In other words, we had to stop opening the door to strange people. So we locked it. And during the last week, we pretty much spent, I would say, 80% of the time in that cellar, because we could hear in the distance the Russian katyushas. You know, those are the big guns. And I remember on one of the mornings, just two, three days before the Russians came, we had a wooden sled that we would play with. And I remember using the sled, lying on my stomach, and sledding up to this thing, which turned out to be later a grenade, an-- an-- an unexploded grenade. Maybe it would never have exploded, and then, again, maybe it would. And of course, I-- I picked it up, looked at it lying on my stomach on the sled, and someone yelled, throw that away. I don't know who it was. It was an adult-sounding voice. So some of the grenades had already landed there. And at night, you could see red in the distance. You know, it was coming close. You could hear it, and the ground was vibrating. We all had to put our ears to the ground to see how close this was. And so on one of our nights, while we were among the potato sacks trying to sleep, each with our clothes on, as many as we could put on, somehow in the early morning I remember I looked up, and the-- the lid-- where you went into the cellar was there's a lid, which is the floor of the upstairs. And then you come down some huge steps, and you're in this little cellar. And the first thing I see is this lid is coming open. And it's obviously very, very early because the color of this-- the light is-- is like a snowy day before the snow and early, early. And I see these bayonets. And they're talking something we can't understand. We all looked at each other. And they knew one word, which was raus, out, meaning come out. Well, we were afraid to come out of there with the bayonets. We're not talking just the gun but the stab them thing on the front there. And we all got out. And my mother thought, now I'm going to be able to reciprocate to the Subitskis by making them safe from the Russians so they won't be treated like the rest of the German perpetrators. That was the next danger. And the first thing she had to establish was, how do we communicate with the Russians here to explain who we are, that we're-- that we're Jews left over.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you-- when did the Russians come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: January '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, Solzhenitsyn's army was in that very region, I read many years later. I was very surprised. And we all came out of there staring at odd uniforms, odd gibberish, bayonets. There was a very fortunate thing. The Russians were always very kind to children. And the first thing they did was offer us-- and I don't know where they got this-- pieces of chicken. And my mother said, don't eat that, in German, this may be poisoned. And so we didn't eat it, but we were terribly hungry. And they started pulling their sleeves up-- and I'm sure you may have heard this from other people-- uree, uree. Well, that was close to the German word for Uhr, which is U-H-R, and they were trying to look for watches. And they had these watches strapped on their arms clear up to here. I don't know how many you need to tell time, but that was loot. And we gave them whatever we had. A couple of people maybe had a watch or two still. These were not people of that kind of means to begin with. And my mother tried to say to them-- I don't know. Remember, we couldn't talk Russian. She knew some snippets of Polish from having had Polish help in the region where she was born. They employed a lot of Polish people who had stayed in the regions, just like the German Jews stayed in the region when it was Polish some of the time. But that doesn't mean you speak their language. There was always a language difference. And then suddenly she remembered a word, being called that by the Polish children, and it was Zhid, which is an later supposedly forbidden term for Jew for the-- for the Russians, happens to be the same word. Many of their words are the same. But it had not too much effect, it turned out. They did take her, though, to their headquarter man, and it luckily was that he happened to be a Russian Jew from Kazakhstan. And he said, I'll try to help you all I can, but all Jews are dead. My mother said, no, no, no, no, no. I have to explain to you. I have to get back to Berlin, I have to, and so on. And this was conducted in half German-- well, she could only do the German. He didn't know any other languages. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he poked along in his Yiddish and told us that he came from a region where the women were veiled-- some other world altogether-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? Samarkand ?] or someplace. I don't know, regions that were remote. And he said, you know, it's not going to be safe for all you people here. And she tried to explain these people had saved us, and we wanted them to be treated well. And-- and he said, I have no control, because I stay here just a short time, and I have to move on and then come the next rush of people. Well, the next rush of people were not the same kind of people. That was-- and they-- they were the Mongolians. That was another grouping altogether. It's a whole other ethnic world of Russia. And they did not listen to anyone. There were no Jews among them. It was a-- a fiasco. And we had to walk. And that was the grouping of people that divided us, if I remember right. First of all, they-- they grabbed the 16-year-old Kristal, the daughter who had gotten the pin from my mother, and dragged her screaming out of the place. The second thing was that the 15 or 14-year-old boy was dragged away, and we never saw either one of them again. Then they told us to walk a ways and put us up in some other rooms. And I remember my mother was in a bed-- in a bed that was with sheets and all properly made up, and the rest of us were sitting in that same room. And she was having a terrible gall bladder attack. And in march these people. And-- and we must have been-- I don't know if we had remained there where we were in the first place and we're were just in a different house-- hut thing. I don't know, maybe we were even back in the same one. I don't know. And I see my mother lying there, and these-- these military Russians come in and drag her from her bed in her nightclothes and my mother screaming and yelling. And-- and we females are all sitting there not knowing what to do. I have to quit. I can't tell that. You have to cut it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to resume. We had to have a momentary pause at the request of the survivor. OK, Evy, so what happened? Your mother was dragged away from you. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't know if they were going to murder her. You know, my mind wasn't adult enough to know anything about, say, rape or any of this. These are all things that children feel an underlying threat about, but they don't really know it in a conscious sense. And all I know is my mother was gone. And immediately, Mama Subitski began to pray all kinds of prayers and-- that I wasn't familiar with about praying at this point anymore. It's-- that was long in-- in the past. And she said, she will be back. She will be back. The following evening she came back in the door. I mean, we just couldn't believe that since none of the others had come back. These details really were never revealed by her. But I assure you that thereafter, when the Russians separated us from the Subitskis, we learned-- yeah, in the last decade I found out that it was a direct Stalin order. If they speak German, treat them like Germans. See, we did not know these things. So they didn't believe who we were. The only people who believed us were periodically meeting Russian Jewish officers. Fortunately, there was a good number of these. It nevertheless happened that we were separated from them, just in the simple sorting of people. You here. You here. Everybody's afraid enough. They're not going to ask why. And my mother's attempts were futile. She never succeeded in getting any help for them or to keep us together. We wanted to stay with-- together with them so that nothing would happen to them. We didn't know if they were going to shoot civilians or what. But we soon found out, after a little bit of marching and this and that, that we found out two things-- that Russians drink like fish. And they were getting huge quantities of vodka just to be able to deal with what they had to deal with in the army. Probably this is common in other countries, too. If there's a war, that's what you feed them-- cigarettes and vodka. And the-- the other thing was that they consequently were drunk most of the hours that they were awake.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we would-- we would go on foot in the snow with people we didn't know, other German civilians that they were rounding up, and they would say, this is where we camp. Sometimes it was in the snow, and sometimes it was inside a place where the plates were still standing on the table where other people had left their meal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how we came, among other places, to a place like Nemmersdorf. I remember this terrible fear thing. My mother would always-- whatever room-like thing we slept in-- and we were not with other people then-- bolting the door and putting a chair under the-- under the door handle and all this. And she always kept me exactly close to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nemmersdorf was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nemmersdorf was still in East Prussia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that a town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- no, it was a village. That village had-- there-- there had been a dispute whether the Germans had came in and had murdered all the Russians that had already captured the territory. It went back and forth several times during the-- the months then and previous. And the other argument was that the Russians came in and murdered all the Germans who were in that town. And that whichever murdered whomever in it, the entire village was dead. And the people had been buried in scarcely a foot and a half of dirt with no lime thrown on it or anything. And by the time we got there, if you got a little cut anywhere and you and the dirt connected, you got the disease, which I got and others did too, but I only had it on my legs. It couldn't be transferred from person to person. And I think it's called hoof-and-mouth disease. But it left huge scars where there would just be a hole in your flesh. There would be no bleeding. You could look right down to your bone. And my mother traded in desperation some-- a ring that she still had for potassium permanganate crystals that we would dilute carefully two crystals at a time and use it. Tear some bedding apart, which you had no way to keep clean anyway, and wrap those around, because the flies liked to sit on there. That I remember real well because I had so many of them. And it took-- it must have taken something like a year and a half for those to heal up on their own. And that occurred because I fell on my knee. I-- we went in and out of a window in one of those houses, because there was so much typhus that had broken out among this German population. Anyway, we were in some camps, here, there. My mother dug her way out of-- with a spoon so we could get away. Then we somehow-- I remember riding on a-- on a cart. That always means horse and cart, nothing motorized. And the Italians had hidden us under-- there were also Italian prisoners of war there, men. And they were very nice to us. But you know, there's always a price for everything. Things like, you come back to Rome with me and marry me and all this kind of stuff. My mother had a terrible time with men. Men were hard to get rid of. But she learned. She told me she fended off the Russians by screaming loud. Not screaming in distress, but when you're talking, screaming, screeching, they would retreat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, kind of like my ex-husband, she said. He would retreat if it got noisy, but otherwise he was a terribly dangerous person eventually. Shouting loud was a good weapon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of any of the camps that you were in in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They had no names. Here's-- we make camp. We string wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you escaped. Your mother escaped with a spoon--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to get you out from under?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of us out of there and we--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I think some Italian servicemen who were trapped there-- and they were released earlier to go home-- helped us by somehow arranging and paying off someone to take us in a wagon with hay. And I remember we had to go some crossings with inspections of Russians, and I remember we had to be under the hay and be quiet. And two-- two of these Italians drove this thing. They must have rented it and paid somebody some money or grabbed it or what. And we had to be very quiet under there, and it took a long time. And somehow we got our way from one place to another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we ended up in-- in Vilna. Vilna--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vilna is the capital of Lithuania. I always have to say it in German, or I get the two countries mixed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know. I never know. I never know any of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your mother there the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because she had to find work. She had to find something to do so we had something to eat. She managed to find Russian Jews-- first a couple that had a brand-new baby who were very religious evidently, which was forbidden by the communists already. And they could not keep me, because keeping you means also that you need to be fed and you need to have room, and they didn't even have enough for themselves. Her father, I believe, was performing circumcisions on Jewish babies for people who still-- who had survived or who were from Russia, had moved forward from Russia, and wanted their children circumcised if they were Jews. And that was forbidden. It was a forbidden activity. I remember this man when I had seen him-- and I had met that family, I got to be there once in a while-- hurrying in his coat and hat and-- and his satchel, like a doctor's little satchel. Well, my mother had to find a place for me, and with the help of these Jewish people found this orphanage in which were the remnants of Jewish children of all ages where we were parked. The trouble was that they took one look at me-- that wasn't so bad, the look. But in the first conversation, they were just in a state of horror because they said, Nemetskiy, Nemetskiy, you know, as if I was faking my way into this orphanage, that I was German. That means German in Russian. That means German. And most of us spoke Russian besides Yiddish. And I-- I kept trying to say what it was, and I couldn't. I didn't speak Yiddish, but it's similar to German. It was a big mess. I tried to explain. There was no one there to help me explain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now I was in this room with some other language. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And these kids were defiant and said that I was German, and all the people had been killed by the Germans. This was when I first started to get some kind of a feeling about what really had happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had told me, two days before the Russians came in that little sharecropper's hut, what was with us, who we were, what did it mean. I didn't understand a word she said. It seemed outrageous.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, because people are something else, they had to be killed? What was this? It made no sense. I didn't even know what killed meant, let alone religion. What do you mean religion? We look like the other people. What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew all that by then. And I knew this was a Jewish orphanage. I knew their parents had gotten killed. I had learned a lot in that time. I knew the people in the camps were Germans who hated us. And my mother had been asked to spy on the other prisoners there. Turn in ten Nazis a-- a-- a week, and we'll give you a cabbage and you'll get to live in grand style. But she wouldn't do it because her ethics said, I don't know these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-mm. Couldn't tell you. It changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long were you in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know that either. I just know we had-- we all had our heads shaved because of the lice, the bed bugs. And I had one little girl who must have felt sorry for me. Her name was Zippella, which means in Yiddish little-- like corner, you know? If you had a cover like a pillowcase, this is the zippella in Yiddish. But in German, it's Zipfel, which means corner. So you see always the similarity? And I would guess what she was saying. Zippella, I thought, was a boy for days on end. And some other boy thought I was a boy. You know, we all had the same look. And Zippella sort of looked after me, would say, come on, come on, give me your piece of soap. They just gave you a piece of soap when you came in here. We didn't get baths, but we had soap. That would be Russian. You got one piece, but not the other. You got a car, but no tires. And I said, I need that soap. And she said, no, no, we have to fill the cracks, the bed bugs. I still remember the smell of dead bed-- bed bugs. It's a sweet, disgusting smell. And she said, the big thing is coming. The big day is coming. We'll share what soap we have left. But we must fill the cracks to keep the bed bugs out. The minute the lights went out, the things were-- were a sea of them. You could feel them under your feet, in your bed, everywhere. And of course they'd bite you, as did the-- the lice and the scabies and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, we're going to have a ceremony soon-- it was the 1st of May, big holiday with the Russians-- and we're going to be red pioneers, you know? First there was the-- the Hitler Youth or the BDM, then came the Red-- Young Pioneers, Young Pioneers, and then comes the Girl Scouts and Boy Scouts. I no longer know where I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got this orange scarf. The color alone was enough to make you participate in this horrible, drab world of nothing. You had on a white blouse with this beautiful scarf. You had to learn how to tie the knot right. You got that thing once a year, and then you gave it back. Then you, again, had nothing to wear. And we had food. But there were always kids who would steal yours faster than you could eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it was a strange world. But I always remember this little blonde Zippella, who said, don't you know anything? Well, she-- I'd say, well, how do you know all these things? And she said because they-- she lived in the forests. So her family was killed, and she was the remnant of leftover Jewish Russian child or a Lithuanian child or whatever she really might have been. I don't even know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my mother heard of another job, she went to work for a Russian woman judge, also had a baby. Oh, the other Jewish family finally got arrested. That's how that flew up. Somebody betrayed them as being-- practicing religion with the father. And I happened to be there on the day this happened. I still see the woman. She had pitch-black hair with a great big braid on the-- like a crown on her head, and with the little baby and the husband and the father. And my mother had to get out of there. I had somehow found my way over there by myself. I ran away from that orphanage twice, and it had to do with shaving the heads. And if you be absent, maybe you wouldn't get your head shaved. Of course, the lice drove you crazy. But anyway, she went to work for Ronia, the woman judge, who had to condemn so many each week, and I'm still at the orphanage. Then my mother got sick, only I didn't know that. The hospital was right behind the orphanage, but no one said. You know, this is Russia. If you ever saw you know, chance meetings come back again. My mother didn't show up for what seemed like months, but it was probably six weeks or so or something. I just figured it was over. She was working for the Russian judge and the baby, and the husband was an actor. The judge would cry herself in her bed every Sunday to make up for all the wrongly condemned people, because it wasn't a true court. It was-- you know, this is how you secure labor. All of the soldiers that came back from World War II got condemned in those courts because there was no work for them. And this is how you get Siberian cities built that you see in today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day-- I had resigned myself. My mother used to come every other Sunday, or I'd meet her at a park where I'd go down the street from the orphanage. I remember we'd been to a movie. I had never seen a film. We-- that meant a Tarzan movie on a primitive little screen, kind of like what we have right here, and we sat on the floor. And it was Tarzan without sound, Tarzan swimming-- swinging from vines. And we came down the street, and what was so horrid, it was always, the orphans are coming. It was disgusting. The orphans must have been a disgusting thing. And I see this little figure kind of dragging the feet with the baby carriage, the-- that woman's baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stop-- and I was actually not with the group. I never was with any group anywhere. And I looked. And finally, we met up and I said, is that you, mommy? I thought you were dead. I know I've said that sentence before. It happened several times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she, as it turned out, had had lockjaw and was very, very sick. And of course, the Russians had no way to treat their own. Hospital in Russia was conscripted to death. No one went to hospitals because you'd never leave again, only dead as a corpse. And in fact, the property behind the orphanage was-- they'd lay them out there after they would die. But we were used to that. Just like prior to that, I had gotten used to seeing shrunken bodies in the snow. You know, you just get used to it. Or a woman who appeared to have her head cut off. That was not unusual.
